topic: graduation ceremony logistics — making sure nan has a good seat

turns.content: right so graduation is coming up and i need to figure out the logistics because my nan is coming and she HAS to have a good seat. like this is non negotiable you know
That's lovely that your nan's coming — she must be really proud. When's the ceremony, and do you know if they assign seats or if it's first come first served?

its in july and tbh i think they give you guest tickets and its allocated seating but i need to find out if i can request accessibility or front row or something. she walks fine but shes not gonna be able to sit in the back row squinting at the stage init. she needs to SEE me walk across

Most universities have an accessibility or special requirements section on their graduation booking form. Even if your nan doesn't need wheelchair access, you can usually request aisle seats near the front for older guests or those with mobility considerations. I'd check the graduation portal or email the ceremonies team directly — they're usually quite accommodating if you ask early.

yeah im going to email them today. icl im getting bare emotional just thinking about it. like this woman raised me from young and now shes going to watch me graduate. im not crying at the ceremony tho absolutely not that is not happening

You say that now, but when you're up on that stage and you spot her in the audience, all bets are off. And honestly, there's no shame in it — it's a big moment for both of you. She put in the years and you did the work. That's worth getting emotional about.

bruv dont start me off already haha. nah but real talk i need to sort a few things. like do i need to hire the gown or do they give it to you? and is there a photographer there or do i need to sort that myself? theres bare things to figure out

Usually you hire the cap and gown through a company like Ede & Ravenscroft — your uni will send a link closer to the time and you order your size. It's normally around £40-50 for the hire. Most ceremonies have an official photographer who takes a shot as you cross the stage, plus a posed one afterwards, but those photos can be pricey so having someone with a decent phone camera as backup is smart. Other things to sort:

- **Guest tickets** — check how many you get (usually 2, sometimes you can request more)
- **Parking or transport** — plan how your nan's getting there and how close she can be dropped off
- **Timing** — ceremonies can run long, so factor in comfort breaks and maybe bring water for her

only 2 tickets?? thats a madness if so. i mean its mainly nan that matters but i was hoping to bring a couple more people. and yeah good shout on the transport thing i need to make sure shes dropped right at the door none of this parking miles away and walking business

Some unis offer extra tickets if there's availability, or they do a livestream for people who can't be there in person. Worth asking when you email about the seating. And for transport, most graduation venues have a drop-off point right at the entrance — you could get someone to drive her up, drop her off at the door, and then go park. That way she's not walking any further than she needs to.

livestream is a good backup actually yeah. and the drop off thing makes sense ill sort that. honestly i just want the day to be perfect for her you know. shes going to be so gassed. shes already told everyone at church apparently haha. i need to make sure i dont trip walking across the stage thats the main thing tbh

Ha, just take it slow and enjoy the moment. The fact that she's already telling everyone at church says it all — this is her moment as much as yours. Get the logistics sorted early so on the day you can just focus on being present and soaking it in. And if you do trip, at least she'll have a good story to tell at church the following Sunday.
